summary: This notebook implements a minimal submission strategy for a text generation competition by generating essays entirely from randomly sampled words rather than training a model. The author splits the test set into three equal parts and applies distinct generation rules: two parts use a prompt template that includes the topic, while the middle part appends a specific "grade 9" exploit prompt to a random word sequence. The approach demonstrates how simple heuristic prompting and strategic test-set splitting can serve as a quick baseline when model training is impractical. Readers should note that competition submissions can sometimes be competitive through clever prompt formatting and targeted exploitation rather than complex architectures.
competition_flow: Reads test topics and a word list, generates three different types of random-word-based essays for different thirds of the test set, and saves them as a CSV submission.
data_reading: Loads test data and sample submission via pd.read_csv, and reads a word list from a local text file using standard Python file I/O.
insights: Simple prompt engineering and strategic test-set splitting can serve as an effective baseline for text generation competitions without requiring model training.
